description: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra displaying the elemental composition and chemical state distribution of surface oxide films formed on Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels (LDSs) after 7200 s potentiostatic polarization in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The figure comprises four rows of high-resolution spectra: Fe 2p₃/₂ (a-d), Mn 2p₃/₂ (a₁-d₁), Al 2p (a₂-d₂), and O 1s (a₃-d₃), comparing four samples with different grain sizes achieved via solution treatment at varying temperatures: S0 (as-rolled), S950 (950°C, fine-grained ~8.7 μm), S1050 (1050°C), and S1150 (1150°C, coarse-grained ~32 μm). The Fe 2p₃/₂ spectra reveal Fe²⁺ (709.8 eV), Fe³⁺_ox (711.5 eV), and Fe³⁺_hy (713.3 eV), with S950 uniquely exhibiting a Fe²⁺_sat satellite peak (715.0 eV) corresponding to FeO, Fe₂O₃, and FeOOH. Mn 2p₃/₂ spectra show Mn²⁺ (640.4 eV), Mn⁴⁺ (641.8 eV), and Mn²⁺_sat (646.2 eV) in S0, S1050, and S1150, attributed to Mn(OH)₂ and MnO₂, whereas S950 displays only the Mn²⁺ oxidation state. Al 2p spectra show a single Al³⁺ peak (74.1 eV) from Al₂O₃ and Al(OH)₃. O 1s spectra deconvolute into O²⁻ (529.7 eV), OH⁻ (531.6 eV), and H₂O (533.2 eV). The spectra demonstrate that S950 forms a dense, highly protective Al-rich oxide film (90.17 at% Al, 25.2 nm thickness) with low defect density (ND 0.26×10²¹/cm³), while S1150 exhibits reduced Al content (~69.91 at%) and higher Fe/Mn oxide incorporation. This supports the conclusion that fine-grained microstructures with high grain boundary density promote preferential Al dissolution and rapid formation of continuous protective oxide films, whereas coarse grains result in discontinuous, defective films with inferior corrosion resistance.
